questions.q: A decision problem is decidable if:
options: A heuristic algorithm can solve it in reasonable time
A Turing machine exists that halts and gives the correct yes/no answer for every input
Humans can understand the problem intuitively
A non-deterministic machine can solve it in polynomial time
questions.explanation: A decidable problem requires a decider: a Turing machine that always terminates with the correct answer. Heuristics and human understanding are informal; polynomial time concerns complexity, not decidability. A non-deterministic machine does not grant decidability if it loops on some inputs. Decidability is about existence of a terminating algorithm.

questions.q: Rice's Theorem states that any non-trivial property of the language recognized by a Turing machine is:
options: Always decidable using a decider
Undecidable (no algorithm can determine if an arbitrary Turing machine has that property)
Decidable in exponential time
Solvable by a non-deterministic machine in polynomial time
questions.explanation: Rice's Theorem proves that checking non-trivial properties of recursively enumerable languages (properties true of some but not all Turing-recognizable languages) is undecidable. Examples include 'Does M accept a finite language?' or 'Is L empty?'. A non-trivial property cannot be mechanically verified for all Turing machines. Trivial properties (always true or always false) are vacuously decidable.

questions.q: Recursively enumerable languages are also called:
options: Regular languages
Context-free languages
Turing-recognizable languages
Decidable languages
questions.explanation: Recursively enumerable (RE) languages are exactly those recognizable by a Turing machine, hence 'Turing-recognizable'. They are weaker than decidable languages, as a recognizer need not halt on rejected strings. Regular and context-free languages are proper subsets of RE languages. 'Decidable' is a stronger property (both the language and its complement are RE).

questions.q: A reduction from problem A to problem B means:
options: A is easier than B
An algorithm solving B can be used to solve A
A can be converted to B in polynomial time
B can be solved by ignoring A entirely
questions.explanation: A reduction from A to B (written A ≤ B) means a solution for B can be transformed into a solution for A. If B is decidable, then A is decidable via reduction. If A is undecidable, then B must be undecidable. Reductions preserve properties (decidability, NP-completeness) in the direction of the reduction. Polynomial time is relevant for complexity, not decidability.

questions.q: The Halting Problem is undecidable because:
options: We have not yet found a fast enough algorithm
Computers lack sufficient memory
It is impossible to determine for all Turing machines whether they halt, as proven by diagonalization
The problem requires infinite input
questions.explanation: The undecidability of the Halting Problem is proven rigorously via diagonalization. Assuming a decider H for the Halting Problem leads to a logical contradiction when H is applied to itself. This proof is independent of algorithmic sophistication or computing resources; it is a fundamental limitation. Limitations on memory or speed would not resolve this inherent undecidability.

questions.q: The complement of a Turing-recognizable language is:
options: Always Turing-recognizable
Always decidable
Sometimes Turing-recognizable, depending on the language
Never Turing-recognizable unless the original language is decidable
questions.explanation: If language L is Turing-recognizable and its complement is also Turing-recognizable, then L is decidable. Conversely, if L is merely Turing-recognizable (and undecidable), its complement is generally not Turing-recognizable. The only exception is when both L and its complement are Turing-recognizable, making L decidable by dovetailing the two recognizers.

questions.q: What is the relationship between decidability and Turing-recognizability?
options: All decidable languages are Turing-recognizable, but not vice versa
Turing-recognizable and decidable languages are the same class
No decidable language is Turing-recognizable
Decidability is defined only for non-Turing-recognizable languages
questions.explanation: If a language is decidable, a decider (halting Turing machine) exists, so a recognizer also exists. Thus every decidable language is Turing-recognizable. The converse fails: the Halting Problem language is Turing-recognizable but undecidable. Decidability is a stronger property, requiring both the language and its complement to be Turing-recognizable.

questions.q: The set of Turing-recognizable languages is:
options: Finite
Countably infinite
Uncountably infinite
Empty
questions.explanation: Each Turing machine is a finite string over a finite alphabet, so there are countably infinitely many Turing machines. Since each Turing machine recognizes one language, there are countably infinitely many Turing-recognizable languages. This is fewer than the uncountably infinite set of all languages over an alphabet, proving that some languages are unrecognizable.

questions.q: To prove a language L is undecidable, a common technique is:
options: Show that L is regular but not context-free
Perform a reduction from a known undecidable problem (like the Halting Problem) to L
Demonstrate that L is infinite
Prove L is Turing-recognizable
questions.explanation: If a known undecidable problem A reduces to L, then L must be undecidable; otherwise A would be decidable via L's decider. This reduction technique leverages known undecidability results. Being regular/context-free, infinite, or Turing-recognizable does not establish undecidability. Undecidability specifically concerns the absence of a halting algorithm.

questions.q: A language L is co-Turing-recognizable if:
options: L is Turing-recognizable
The complement of L is Turing-recognizable
L is decidable
L is the union of two Turing-recognizable languages
questions.explanation: Co-Turing-recognizable means the complement of L is Turing-recognizable. A language is decidable if and only if it is both Turing-recognizable and co-Turing-recognizable. Union of two Turing-recognizable languages is Turing-recognizable, not co-Turing-recognizable. Complement and co-recognizability are dual properties in the hierarchy of recognizability.
